title: This is boring, but it makes millions
description: Positions unconventional advice as proven results driver. Uses named framework to structure actionable steps, reframes discomfort as necessary growth, and builds credibility through personal success. Ends with engagement hook. Ideal for sharing counterintuitive strategies or habit...
content:
The following advice is boring.

But it [generates impressive result for me] every [time period].

If you really want to [achieve desired outcome], focus on what I call the '[named framework].'

Work out what you need to do every single [time period] to experience [result 1] and [result 2].

Depending on the [context], every [time period] we do [example action 1], [example action 2], etc.

[Briefly explain how to apply framework].

Because here's the thing...

When you're [at specific stage], it's all about [activity]. And it's [positive emotion] because you're [experiencing positive thing].

But at some point, you [engage in beneficial action].

So, you've got to embrace a little bit of [reframed negative feeling].

All it means is that [high-level positive outcome] and you have a [named framework] that is guaranteed to [drive specific desirable outcome] as long as you want it to.

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: X Is No Different Than Y (Other Than…)
description: Simplifies complex new concepts by comparing them to familiar ones, then highlighting key differences. Uses analogy to reduce cognitive load and make emerging trends accessible. Perfect for thought leadership posts explaining industry innovations, new technologies, or evolving business practices to your network.
content: [Emerging concept/trend] is no different than [established concept/trend], except for X [notable differences/pain points]:

title: A Storied Comeback
description: Creates compelling transformation stories by contrasting a challenging starting point with remarkable success, then revealing the key principle behind the change. Uses curiosity gap psychology to drive engagement through the "How did they do it?" question, making readers want to discover the solution.
content:
In [year], [entity/individual] faced [challenging situation].

By [later year], they achieved [extraordinary turnaround/result]. How did they do it?

[Core concept or principle].

title: The nightmare behind a shiny facade (a lesson in storytelling)
description: Behind-the-scenes vulnerability template that contrasts public success with private struggles. Uses social proof and relatability to build trust, then offers a solution. Leverages authenticity bias and the gap between perception vs reality to engage audiences seeking genuine connection.
content:
Ever wondered what lies behind the glittering façade of [industry/profession/role]? 

Let me take you behind the scenes.

[Short time ago], I spoke with [describe person and their impressive credentials]. 

From the outside, [brief description of their success and lifestyle]. 

But here's what you don't see:

[Share a vulnerable admission/predicament they’re in]. 

Despite [brief description of achievements], they're facing [key challenges]. 

Here’s what they said:

"[Direct quote from the conversation]"

This story isn't unique. 

It's the untold reality many face in [industry/profession], where [brief description of common misconceptions].

I've [walked this path myself/seen it unfold like this before]. 

Sometimes, [positive outcomes]. 

But often, [common negative outcomes].

Then one day, you realise [reflective thought or decision].

For those feeling the same, looking for [what the reader might be seeking], I’ve created something for you. Check out this [resource/tool/link]: [Link]

**Note: Avoid revealing the identity (or any sensitive details) of any individuals without their consent.

title: If It Worked For Them, It Can Work For You
description: Builds credibility by sharing proven methods that deliver consistent results. Uses social proof from client success to increase trust and positions you as an expert who can replicate outcomes. Perfect for coaches, consultants, and service providers wanting to showcase their expertise.
content:
These 3 [habits/routines/hacks] help me [reach desired outcome] routinely:

(and they’ve worked for all my clients)

title: The Unconventional Hire
description: Creates intrigue by revealing an unconventional approach that defied expectations and led to success. Uses curiosity gap and contrast principle to hook readers who want to learn the surprising strategy that worked when traditional methods were avoided.

I didn't [conduct expected action 1] nor did I [conduct expected action 2].

Now, [person or scenario has reached desirable outcome].

But [unexpected outcome or obstacle].

Leverages curiosity gap and social proof to drive comments and discussions. Works best for thought leadership content where you can defend an unpopular but defensible position.

title: What are the costs of inaction? (FOMO)
description: This template creates urgency around adopting better habits by contrasting negative behaviors with positive alternatives. Uses loss aversion psychology and social proof through "ultimate hack" framing. Perfect for coaches, consultants, and thought leaders sharing actionable advice that positions them as experts.
content:
The ultimate [topic/field] hack: 

[Strategy/Habit/Desired attribute].

Don’t:

→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 1]
→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 2]
→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 3]

Instead:

→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 1]
→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 2]
→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 3]

Every day you put off [key positive action] is another day selling yourself short.

Your future [goal/outcome] depends on it.

[Call to action and/or question for audience].

[Relatable text image to act as a ‘billboard’ for your post]

title: Educational Asset
description: Creates urgency and accessibility by combining time scarcity with skill acquisition. Appeals to busy professionals seeking quick wins and immediate value. The short timeframe removes barriers to commitment while positioning the content as efficient and results focused.

[impressive results]

Uses exclusion psychology to make the advice feel more accessible and less intimidating than traditional methods.

title: Something Was Off

Perfect for entrepreneurs, career changers, or anyone who achieved results through unconventional paths rather than traditional credentials.

title: I Just Knew

Uses the psychological principle of curiosity gap to compel readers to continue reading. Perfect for sharing lessons learned from mistakes, failures, or pivotal moments that led to significant negative outcomes.

the moment [trigger event].

title: Need Good Thing (Without Bad Thing)?
description: Creates urgency by addressing a specific need, then promises multiple solutions while removing common barriers. Uses the psychological principle of loss aversion by highlighting what readers want to maintain or achieve, making them more likely to engage with the content for fear of missing valuable insights.
content:

X ways to [achieve said outcome] (without [obstacle or frustration]):

description: Leverages authority bias by citing respected figures, then bridges their wisdom to your industry with specific examples. [Example 2]

[Example 3]

[Example 4]

The challenging state hook immediately captures attention while the timeline creates anticipation for the resolution story.

[Time period] ago, I was at [starting point].

description: Vulnerability storytelling template for entrepreneurs sharing failure stories. Uses the hero's journey structure to build authentic connection and credibility. Leverages psychological principles of relatability and social proof to inspire struggling founders while humanizing success stories.

title: The Midwit Razor is (still) undefeated
description: Exposes rebranded advice by applying the Midwit Razor to reveal that trendy tactics are just old concepts with new names. Builds credibility by cutting through hype, positioning you as a clear thinker who sees through marketing noise.

[Familiar concept] has been getting a rebrand for months.

[New name 1], [New name 2], [New name 3], ...

Then every week I read [content type] pushing new '[rebrand name]’ tactics.

"[Recycled advice 1]"
"[Recycled advice 2]"
"[Recycled advice 3]"
"[Recycled advice 4]"

The Midwit Razor kicks in:

Looking at these [rebrand name] tactics, what would the "idiot" and the "genius" both agree on?

Answer: "It's just [the original concept]"

That's 90% of the [rebrand name] advice out there.

These are [decades/years]-old [original discipline] tactics repackaged as revolutionary findings.

[Briefly share your take].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: The Real Crusher
description: Creates provocative posts by contrasting an overlooked soft skill against commonly desired resources like money or connections. Uses scarcity psychology and counterintuitive thinking to challenge assumptions about success factors. Effective for thought leadership content that reframes priorities and sparks debate.
content: Lack of [important trait] [kills/stops] more [goals/professionals] than lack of [commonly sought resource] ever will.

title: Only ONE person reads your content
description: A contrarian reframing template that challenges conventional wisdom by positioning an alternative approach as more aligned with human values. Uses pattern interrupts, social proof, and outcome comparisons to shift audience perspective. Leverages the psychological principle that people are drawn...
content:
Stop [doing the common thing]. Start [doing alternative thing].

We've become obsessed with [behaviour].

[Parallel phrase - e.g. “Chasing X instead of Y. Optimising for Z, not W”].

But [system] doesn't [work according to the story you’re telling yourself].
It doesn't [feel/see/value] your [human quality].

[Group/method] [do/does].

And here's what most people forget:

[Core reframe - a counterintuitive way of seeing the audience or the task].

[One sentence that makes the reframe concrete and visceral].

When you [do the wrong thing]:
- [Negative outcome 1]
- [Negative outcome 2]
- [Negative outcome 3]

When you [do the right thing]:
- [Positive outcome 1]
- [Positive outcome 2]
- [Positive outcome 3]

[System] will change tomorrow.
[The platforms/tools/rules] will change next quarter.

But [the human constant] remains constant.

And the best part?

When you stop [chasing the system] and start [doing alternative], [system] often rewards you anyway.

Because [platforms/markets/audiences] ultimately want: [simple thing].

[Key takeaway].

title: The AIDA copywriting formula
description: Reframes unrealistic expectations by breaking down overwhelming goals into specific, actionable steps. Uses loss aversion and achievability bias to motivate immediate action while setting realistic timelines. Perfect for coaches, consultants, and thought leaders addressing common industry frustrations.
content:
It’s impossible to [achieve a common goal] on/in [platform/tool/group] today.

Instead, here's what's achievable today:

- You can [specific action] to [achieve specific goal].
- You can [specific action] to [achieve specific goal].
- You can [specific action] to [achieve specific goal].
- You can [specific action] to [achieve specific goal].

It’ll only take you [realistic time investment].

However, in [relatively short time frame], you’ll have:

- [Desirable outcome]
- [Desirable outcome]
- [Desirable outcome]

Don’t say:

"I want to [achieve common goal] on/in [platform/tool/group] today"

Instead, say:

"I want to [engage in specific daily action] today"

Pick [new strategy for success] over [common but less effective strategy].

P.S. [Foster follower engagement with a personal sign-off]

title: The What, Why, How
description: Targets people facing specific challenges by offering expert solutions. Creates immediate relevance through direct problem identification, builds authority by explaining root causes, then delivers valuable actionable content. Uses problem/solution framework to drive engagement from struggling audiences.
content:
If you’re [struggling with a specific challenge], read this:

The main reason for that is [state reason].

Here are X [content types] that I use to [unlock benefit]:

title: Ever Felt Like This? Same
description: Creates relatability by acknowledging common professional frustrations through rhetorical questions, then builds trust with personal vulnerability. Uses the "me too" principle to establish connection before transitioning into solution or advice. Perfect for coaches, consultants, or thought leaders addressing workplace challenges.
content:
Do you ever feel like [task or role] is [negative emotion or perception]?

Like you’re just [putting up with unfavourable state/outcome], while you long for [better outcome]?

I used to feel this way too.

title: Every Creator Should Use These (Advanced)
description: This template uses the "humble brag" technique to build credibility while appearing modest. It starts with a success, immediately downplays it, then provides valuable actionable advice. The psychological principle leverages vulnerability and authenticity to increase engagement and trust.
content:
I’ve [achieved specific success or milestone] in the last [short timeframe].

But [counterpoint that diminishes the milestone’s value].

[Brief anecdote or lesson learned].

Here’s how to [achieve a specific result/solve a problem]:

[Actionable step 1]

[Clear, practical explanation of the step].

[Actionable step 1]

[Clear, practical explanation of the step].

[Actionable step 1]

[Clear, practical explanation of the step].

[Actionable step 1]

[Clear, practical explanation of the step].

[Reiterate the overarching lesson or insight from the steps].

[Question for audience or call to action].

[Image or video that matches the post]

title: What are the costs of inaction? (FOMO)
description: This template creates urgency around adopting better habits by contrasting negative behaviors with positive alternatives. Uses loss aversion psychology and social proof through "ultimate hack" framing. Perfect for coaches, consultants, and thought leaders sharing actionable advice that positions them as experts.
content:
The ultimate [topic/field] hack: 

[Strategy/Habit/Desired attribute].

Don’t:

→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 1]
→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 2]
→ [Negative action/experience negative consequence 3]

Instead:

→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 1]
→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 2]
→ [Positive action/unlock desirable outcome 3]

Every day you put off [key positive action] is another day selling yourself short.

Your future [goal/outcome] depends on it.

[Call to action and/or question for audience].

[Relatable text image to act as a ‘billboard’ for your post]

title: Meaningful Achievement
description: Creates contrast between past indifference and current excitement about achievements to demonstrate personal growth. Uses the psychological principle of before/after comparison to show evolution in mindset or effort, making the milestone feel more meaningful and relatable to audiences.
content:
I reached [milestone/achievement][short time ago].
When I reached [prior milestone/achievement] I wasn’t bothered.

But it’s different this time. Because [reason related to increased effort or change].

title: Tumultuous Tale
description: Creates compelling success stories by contrasting humble beginnings with major achievements, then teasing the complex journey behind the success. Uses curiosity gap psychology to hook readers who want to learn about overcoming obstacles and challenges on the path to success.
content:
[Individual/Company] was [give brief context] with [describe idea or endeavour].

X years later, they’ve [achieved big goal or milestone].

But their journey was anything but straightforward.

title: Reinforce what people already believe
description: A persuasive template that positions a specific asset as essential for success. Uses authority positioning and list format to build credibility. Perfect for consultants, coaches, and service providers to establish expertise while highlighting the value of their offering through structured benefits presentation.
content:
The most important [specific asset/trait/quality] any [target audience] can have is an [asset/person/team/strategy/tool] who/that [prioritises desirable trait/quality].

[Key quality or action 1]

[Key quality or action 2]

[Key quality or action 3]

[Key quality or action 4]

[Key quality or action 5]

[Summarise the advantage or importance of having such an asset/person/team/strategy/tool].

title: I’ve Done The Hard Work So You Don’t Have To
description: Creates urgency and value by contrasting the author's lengthy, expensive learning journey with a promise to teach the same skill quickly and presumably for free. Leverages social proof through personal investment while positioning the author as an expert who can shortcut the audience's path to mastery.
content: [Skill growth] took me [monetary cost] and [time investment] to master, I’ll teach you in [short time frame]:

title: “What’s the benefit of the benefit?” – Dan Nelken
description: Vulnerability-based template that builds trust by sharing personal struggles, then positions the author as the solution provider. Uses authenticity to connect with audience pain points before transitioning to a soft sales pitch. Leverages relatability and problem-solution psychology.
content:
This [week/month/year] has been tough for me.

[Specific problem faced]

[Specific problem faced]

[Specific problem faced]

[Briefly reflect on struggle].

Sure, I could make excuses, like:

[Current obstacle faced/common excuse people give]

[Current obstacle faced/common excuse people give]

[Current obstacle faced/common excuse people give]

But such is life. No one gets out alive.

[Sum up key reason why for challenges faced].

And for most [target audience], when things go wrong:

[Typical misguided action]

[Typical misguided action]

[Typical misguided action]

But I’m not worried.

Because I [have the specific solution needed to unlock desirable outcome].

And that’s the problem I’m here to solve.

So if you’re [target audience], and you’re tired of [pain point], and want to [unlock benefits]...

[Relevant call-to-action]

And [unlock key desired outcomes].

PS. [Question to prompt reflection or engagement]?

[Image that matches the post]

title: Creative Reframing (Not Enough Creators Do This)
description: A vulnerability-based storytelling template that transforms personal struggles into universal lessons. Uses the "struggle to success" narrative arc to build credibility, then pivots to broader insights. Employs pattern interruption and relatability to engage audiences facing similar challenges, ending with actionable guidance.
content:
[A past struggle you faced].

That’s [statement that emphasises implications of struggle].

But then things finally changed.

This isn’t a post about [specific challenge/topic]. It’s about [topic]. 

[Key takeaway].

Perhaps you’re:

[Chasing goal/pursuit]

[Chasing goal/pursuit]

[Chasing goal/pursuit]

And perhaps you’re:

[Experiencing pain point]

[Experiencing pain point]

[Experiencing pain point]

[Reinforce key takeaway].

[Call to action].

[Relatable text image to act as a ‘billboard’ for your post]

title: Want to craft posts that sell? Try this…
description: Social proof template showcasing client transformation through specific results and expert positioning. Uses success story narrative to build credibility, then transitions to service pitch with urgency tactics. Leverages authority bias and scarcity psychology to drive conversions.
content:
[Timeframe] ago, I signed a client.

The results were amazing

[Impressive result 1]

[Impressive result 2]

[Impressive result 3]

[Briefly explain what prior challenges your client faced – e.g. what they’d tried in the past that didn’t work].

But when they started working with me, they [unlocked key benefit or transformation].

And it wasn’t just luck.

It's based on my:

[List attributes that showcase your dedication and expertise].

My [product or service] ([give price]) helps you [unlock key desirable outcome].

So you can [achieve key result] and get:

[Benefit 1]

[Benefit 2]

[Benefit 3]

[Benefit 4]

[Relevant call to action]

([Brief note on limited-availability or a price increase to encourage people to act now])

[Image that matches the post]

title: How To Keep People Reading (A Storytelling Trick)
description: This template creates compelling personal transformation stories using the "hero's journey" narrative structure. It builds credibility through vulnerability, demonstrates growth over time, and provides actionable takeaways. Perfect for sharing career pivots, business successes, or skill development journeys.
content:
[Time period] ago, I [describe unexpected outcome].

And it all started with [simple action/decision/discovery]. 

Here’s the story:

I began [mention the activity or initiative] back in [relevant time period].

[Briefly reflect on that time, your circumstances, your mindset].

[Mention one or more relatable struggles or challenges you faced].

But I’ve always been drawn to [mention intrinsic motivation or passion].

So I [took specific action].

Around that time, [introduce character or key realisation you had].

[Briefly expand on why this event was significant and how it aligned with your goal].

[Expand how a simple interaction/moment/discovery/decision led to unexpected events or successes – give brief context where necessary].

That’s how I [key turning point – e.g. a moment where things changed for the better].

[List any results/accomplishments/positive outcomes that followed].

But here’s the thing…

[Key takeaway that’s clearer now with hindsight].

Here are a few tactical takeaways I’d suggest for anyone considering a similar path:

[Practical lesson 1]

[Practical lesson 2]

[Practical lesson 3]

[Image that matches the post]

title: An Unconventional Practice
description: Leverages authority and curiosity by revealing unexpected practices of respected figures. Creates intrigue through contrast between status and unconventional methods, then validates the approach by explaining its effectiveness. Builds credibility while challenging assumptions.
content:
The [role/title] of [well-known person/company] used to [surprising or unconventional practice].

Highly unusual but [explain the purpose or effect of the practice].

title: To sell more, acknowledge and resolve objections
description: This template addresses technology adoption fears by acknowledging common concerns, then reframing the narrative with reassurance and benefits. Uses social proof through expert endorsements to build credibility. Perfect for introducing new tools or methods to hesitant professionals.
content:
“I don’t want [common fear or misconception].”

[Reassure reader]

Many [target audience] think [new development in technology/method] will [lead to negative outcome]. 

But don’t worry.

Because [provide a new way to think about it].

- [Briefly explain relevant feature]
- [Briefly explain relevant feature]
- [Briefly explain relevant feature]

And it [unlocks benefit].

[List more relevant benefits]

[State high-level desirable outcome(s)]

Smart [professionals/role] like [Name 1] and [Name 2] are getting involved.

They're using [Product Name] to [carry out action/achieve desirable outcome].

[Relevant thought-provoking question/Call to action]

[Image that matches the post]

title: I Was Lost Until I Found…
description: Creates credibility through vulnerability by sharing past struggles before revealing a transformative solution. Uses the contrast principle to highlight dramatic improvement. Perfect for thought leaders wanting to position expertise while remaining relatable and inspiring action.
content:
I spent years [struggling with X / doing Y ineffectively]. 

Then I discovered [key individual’s strategy/resource/insight] that transformed my 
entire [specific process].

title: Gamify Your Educational Content
description: This template uses curiosity gaps and visual storytelling to drive engagement. Opens with an intriguing hook tied to an image, explains a key concept through examples, highlights practical benefits, and ends with actionable next steps. Perfect for educational content that transforms complex ideas into memorable, shareable posts.
content:
[Follow on from image with a relevant statement or question that piques curiosity – e.g. “You probably won’t guess it.”].

[Introduce and explain key concept from image].

For example, [explain how the key concept informs what’s going on in the image or the choice the reader makes]. (Source: [Credit source(s) if applicable].

[Describe the key benefit(s) of applying key concept].

[Suggest how key concept can be used in other contexts – if this applies to your context].

[Revelant call to action – eg. an event invite or link to an additional content resource].

[Image of thought experiment, challenge or guessing game that relates to key concept from post].

title: A Surefire Way To Improve Your Writing
description: Authority-building template that positions you as an expert by sharing insights, identifying common mistakes, and providing actionable frameworks. Uses social proof and problem/solution psychology to engage audiences seeking professional guidance and practical solutions.
content:
[Opening line that establishes credibility or introduces an insight].

[Concise, impactful statement reinforcing the main idea].

Works for:  

[Relevant context where insight applies 1]  
[Relevant context where insight applies 2]  
[Relevant context where insight applies 3]  

[Statement that draws from your experience in the area].  

Here’s a common mistake I often see:

[Explain common mistake you’ve observed].

I know it’s costing them [specific negative consequence(s)].  

This simple  framework can help:  

[Framework Name]  

[List steps involved].

Here’s an example:

[Give a relatable example of framework being applied].

[Reinforce or reiterate core insight].  

Use this [framework] to [achieve desired outcome(s)].  

[Question for audience or call to action].

[Relatable text image to act as a ‘billboard’ for your post]

title: You're Bad (But It’s Not Your Fault)
description: Shifts blame from personal inadequacy to external circumstances, reducing shame and resistance to change. Creates psychological safety by validating struggles while positioning the author as a solution provider with actionable steps to overcome obstacles.
content:
Your [skill/trait] is lacking because [external factor] let you down.

Follow these X steps and to [unlock benefit]:

title: Unsure what to post? Try this…
description: Contrarian storytelling that builds relatability through shared struggle, then pivots to agency and mindset. Uses social proof and dichotomy to inspire audience reflection on how identical circumstances yield different outcomes based on personal choice and belief systems.
content:
I might get crucified for this, but oh well.

I saw [a piece of content / interaction / moment] recently and it got me thinking.

A lot of us who [shared background or identity] experienced [difficult or formative situation]. [Briefly expand].

A lot of us [second shared experience]. A lot of us [third shared experience - escalates the weight of the circumstances].

Some of us take those experiences and say because of that [negative outcome in life, career, or relationships]. We blame [external cause] for [current result].

Then there are some of us who say [same external cause] happened, but so what. We are going to [declaration of intent] anyway.

I find this genuinely fascinating. How can two people go through the exact same situation and one of them decides it is [outcome A] whilst the other decides it is [outcome B]?

[One or two sentence core belief - what separates the two camps].

[Key takeaway].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: The Unconventional Hire
description: Creates intrigue by revealing an unconventional approach that defied expectations and led to success. Uses curiosity gap and contrast principle to hook readers who want to learn the surprising strategy that worked when traditional methods were avoided.
content:
[Time period] ago, I [took an unconventional action].

I didn't [conduct expected action 1] nor did I [conduct expected action 2].

Now, [person or scenario has reached desirable outcome].

title: 7 Lessons To Success
description: Creates vulnerability-driven storytelling by contrasting past struggles with current success, then delivering valuable lessons. Uses the transformation narrative to build credibility and emotional connection before sharing actionable insights that promise similar results.
content:
When I finished [previous position], I [found myself in an uncomfortable situation].

X years/months later, I [find myself in a better situation].

Here are X key [content type] I've learned along the way to [achieving desirable outcome]: …

title: It’s Not About X, It’s About Y
description: Reframes conventional thinking by challenging surface-level assumptions and revealing deeper truths. Perfect for thought leadership posts that position you as someone who sees beyond obvious solutions. Uses cognitive reframing to make audiences reconsider their beliefs and view you as insightful.
content:
[Common belief or practice] isn't about [traditional focus/parameters].

It's about [deeper purpose or impact].

title: Your POV doesn’t just attract customers
description: Contrarian value positioning template. Challenges conventional metrics by progressively normalizing alternative success measures, then reframes decision-making around actual outcomes. Uses graduated acceptance to build credibility before pivoting to deeper principle. Ideal for thought leadership...
content:
I don't care [about the conventional metric everyone defaults to].

[Scenario 1]? [Short neutral acceptance].
[Scenario 2]? [Short neutral acceptance].
[Scenario 3]? [Slightly warmer acceptance].
[Scenario 4]? [Active encouragement].

I don't [hire/work with/choose] [people/things] to [control the conventional metric].

I [hire/work with/choose] [people/things] to [deliver the real outcome].

And if [the result] is there, I couldn't care less [about the conventional metric].

P.S. It's called [the underlying principle or value].

title: A Challenging Time
description: Creates vulnerability-driven engagement by sharing personal struggles and recovery journeys. Uses emotional storytelling to build authentic connections with audiences who relate to overcoming challenges. Triggers empathy and trust through honest disclosure of difficult experiences and resilience.
content:
[Year/time period] was one of the most challenging times [I’ve/we/company] had. 

[Briefly describe the emotional toll]. [State specific challenge faced]. 

So I [action taken]. [Initial result].

title: My Influential Teacher
description: Leverages social proof and curiosity by sharing wisdom from successful people in your network. Creates credibility through association while building suspense with the "here's what they said" cliffhanger. Perfect for sharing valuable insights while positioning yourself as connected to high achievers.
content:
I have a [friend/colleague/acquaintance] who [impressive accomplishment].

I once asked them [key insight/secret/principle/question]...

Here’s what they said:

title: “I Don’t Have Any Stories To Share”
description: This storytelling template uses social proof and narrative psychology to share wisdom through an intriguing encounter. Opens with curiosity, delivers a key lesson through dialogue, then applies it to relatable scenarios. Perfect for thought leadership posts that educate while building authority.
content:
A few months ago, I met [unique or intriguing person].

[Briefly tease what makes them and the interaction you had interesting]. 

Here’s what they said:

I asked them [question], and they said “[interesting response that sums up or relates to key lesson].”

[Briefly give your take on their response].

[Expand on the lesson further: set up the situation/problem, add relevant context, reveal resulting actions].

[Highlight the key lesson].

In many areas of life, [core idea] applies too:

[Example 1: Relatable scenario].

[Example 2: Relatable scenario].

[Example 3: Relatable scenario].

[Empowering conclusion or actionable takeaway].

title: Time For A Rethink
description: Challenges conventional thinking by presenting a contrarian viewpoint followed by personal transformation story. Uses cognitive dissonance to grab attention, then provides social proof through personal experience. Perfect for thought leadership posts that position you as an innovative problem solver while offering valuable insights to your audience.
content:
We need to rethink [concept/practice]...

This simple shift transformed how I [pursue goal/task] (and may help you too):

[Explain where people often go wrong].

title: It’s Becoming More Popular To…
description: Positions you as an industry expert by identifying flawed trends and their negative outcomes. Creates engagement through contrarian viewpoint that challenges popular but ineffective practices. Uses pattern recognition to build credibility while subtly promoting better alternatives.
content: There’s a rising trend in/on [industry/medium/platform] to [conduct trending behaviour], [get a sub-par outcome] then [give/take misguided belief or action].

title: Mistakes That Cost Dearly
description: Leverages vulnerability and loss aversion to capture attention by promising valuable lessons from costly mistakes. Creates credibility through specific quantified losses and positions the author as someone who learned the hard way, making their advice more trustworthy and relatable.
content:
I made X huge mistakes during my first X months/years of [endeavour]. 

(these cost me [specific quantified losses])

title: Why You Should ‘Show Your Work’ (Sell More With This Trick)
description: Product launch template using social proof and problem-solution framework. Builds credibility through investment disclosure, milestone progression, and customer validation. Uses contrast positioning ("not this, but that") and feature-benefit structure. Leverages success metrics to drive action.
content:
I’ve invested [significant time and/or financial investment] into this idea. 

[Product’s tagline] for [target audience].

[Milestone or initial step you took to get here]

[Milestone or initial step you took to get here]

[Milestone or initial step you took to get here]

We’ve been working closely with [client/customer/target audience members], and I’ve/we’ve identified the key challenges they face:

[Problem 1]

[Problem 2]

[Problem 2]

That’s why I/we created [product name].

This is not [what it’s not]. It’s [brief description of what product is + primary benefit].

Features include:

[Feature 1]

[Feature 2]

[Feature 3]

[Feature 4]

Without this, you’d [briefly describe how alternative methods are inferior or more costly] to get the same results. And the best part?

[Unique benefit 1]

[Unique benefit 2]

Since launching, we’ve seen:

[Key success metric/client result]

[Key success metric/client result]

[Key success metric/client result]

[Key success metric/client result]

This list goes on. Head over to our website [website link] to learn more.

[Image or video that matches the post]

title: How to dispel false beliefs (that stop people from buying)
description: Debunks common industry myths by contrasting widespread beliefs with factual evidence. Uses social proof and authority positioning to challenge misconceptions. Perfect for thought leadership posts that educate audiences and establish expertise in your field.
content:
What [specific group] say:

“[Common misconception framed as a quote].”

What’s true:

- [Fact, stat, or benefit that supports the contrary].
- [Fact, stat, or benefit that supports the contrary].
- [Fact, stat, or benefit that supports the contrary].
- [Fact, stat, or benefit that supports the contrary].

[Bold claim that reinforces core message].

[Empowering takeaway].

title: Apply this thesis hook to almost any domain
description: Contrarian insight template that challenges common assumptions using authority citations and data points. Builds credibility by debunking myths, then redirects audience toward sustainable alternatives. Uses pattern interrupt and social proof to shift mindsets and drive behavior change.
content:
Everyone wants [desirable outcome].
Nobody wants to do what it actually takes.
And I have bad news, because it takes...a LOT.

[Authority/source] finally confirmed that [common assumption] is [wrong/misguided].
[Briefly explain why].

So if [precondition], you likely won't [desired result].

Then there's the question of [related unresolved issue].

[Data source] data shows [surprising finding that contradicts assumption]. Not [expected answer]. [One-line explanation of why].

The logic:

[Core mechanism, stated plainly, first cause].

[Second cause explained]. [Include second data point - if applicable].

You heard that correctly: [punchy restatement of the insight].

And finally, dear [target audience], [common goal] might not be the [desirable thing] you think it is. Another study by [source] found [briefly explain surprising finding].

The rest [alternative reason for the outcome], not because [original assumption].

So stop chasing [false goal].
Build [the real, sustainable goal].

title: What to say when your reader’s heard it all before…
description: Authority building template using social proof and expertise positioning. Shares a proven method through research backed insights and actionable strategies. Uses credibility markers, list format for easy scanning, and examples to build trust before delivering a clear CTA.
content:
I [started endeavour] in [time period]. 

(Steal my method for [achieving desirable outcome])

It's not just about [common assumption]. 

It's about using “[unique strategy name]” to [achieve goal].

After [doing a significant amount of research/analysis], here’s how [eminent figures/companies get desirable results]:

- [Mission/Aim 1]
- [Mission/Aim 2]
- [Mission/Aim 3]

[Briefly reinforce/explain points from above].

[Statement that transitions to actionable advice]:

- [Strategy/Tactic 1]
- [Strategy/Tactic 2]
- [Strategy/Tactic 3]

For example:

[Give example(s) – if appropriate]

[Additional insight/tip]

[Key takeaway]

PS. [Call to action or question for audience]

[Image that matches the post]

title: Not sure what to write about? Try this…
description: Founder origin story template showing scrappy early days vs. current success. Uses contrast, specific metrics, and persistence narrative to build credibility and inspire. Leverages relatability and social proof to establish authority while motivating audience through struggle-to-success arc.
content:
When I was getting started as a [role/creator/professional], I was [doing early version of what you do now and explain key thing you did differently].

I needed [specific number] of [clients/customers/sales] [key reason why].

It was hard. Almost every [sale/win/deal] was [the scrappy, manual way you had to do it].

I literally had [a physical tool or system you used to track progress]. [Describe exactly how it worked - the specific ritual or routine].

It was hard, but [explain what kept you going]. 

And it worked.

Over time, [describe how the flywheel started turning and things got easier].

Today, [describe where you are now - specific number or rate that shows the contrast].

If you're getting started, just know, it gets easier. [Brief insight about why the economics or dynamics shift over time].

But it's all on the other side of [the difficult phase].

[Punchy sign-off].

title: How To Write An Event Invite Post
description: Event promotion template using the "What if" curiosity hook to generate interest. Lists three key benefits while addressing common objections. Builds credibility through qualifications and creates urgency with specific event details. Ideal for coaches, consultants, and educators marketing workshops or seminars.
content:
Want to [achieve a desired goal]?

What if you could [describe relevant scenario]:

[Unlock benefit 1]

[Unlock benefit 2]

[Unlock benefit 3]

All of this – without [undesirable action or obstacle].

[Briefly explain why you or others involved are qualified to speak on the topic – e.g. include achievements, results, or accreditations].

This [event] is designed specifically for [target audience] and focuses on [unique selling point], helping you master [key skill or area of expertise].

[Give any further details – e.g. reinforce a key benefit or allay objections].

Join us on [event date].

At [Location].

[$Price].

[Call to action that makes sense for your event – you may want to include additional details, discounts, or bonuses for people who purchase tickets now].

Link in comments below.

[Image that advertises event with key details]

title: Intriguing Principle
description: Creates curiosity with a bold statement, then promises a solution to a desirable outcome while establishing credibility. Uses the curiosity gap principle to drive engagement by making readers want to learn the "how" behind the intriguing claim.
content:
[Intriguing statement or principle]

How to [achieve desirable outcome]

([credibility booster])

title: What PROMISE are you making to the reader?
description: This template leverages reciprocity and relationship building to showcase professional growth. Users share how helping someone led to unexpected success, demonstrating the power of genuine networking. The vulnerability of admitting struggles builds trust while the structured format creates compelling storytelling.
content:
I [achieved remarkable outcome] [through unexpected means].

[X years/months] ago, I [met/supported someone] at [event/scenario].

They’re also [role/accomplishment/recognition].

[Short time period ago], I reached out to them because:

I [related struggle]

I [related struggle]

I [related struggle]

[Time period ago], [individual/company] told me:

[Briefly explain their question/goal/problem].

I looked back at all the [content/work/actions] that ever [drove the result they wanted] for me from [early milestone] to [current milestone].


There were [X] clear trends:

[Finding 1]
[Finding 2]
[Finding 3]

[Distil a common thread between the findings].

It wasn't [common assumption 1] or [common assumption 2] driving [the result].

It was [the real driver].

This might sound [anticipated objection]. But it's not.

It's actually the [reframe how it's easier/simpler/cheaper than expected].

Because you're just:

[List or state relevant actions/steps/tips].

(+ here's the system I use to [get result] fast: [resource link])


[Question to prompt reflection]?

PS. [Question for audience or call to action].

title: Safe vulnerability vs TERRIFYING vulnerability
description: A raw vulnerability template that goes beyond safe admissions to share genuinely uncomfortable internal doubts. Uses vivid scene-setting and contrast between external success and internal questioning to create deep emotional resonance. The self-deprecating reframe at the end balances the heaviness with relatability, making the post feel authentic rather than performative.
content:
[Time period ago] I watched [vivid image of someone else doing something noteworthy]. Meanwhile, I couldn't shake the [thing pulling me back to my own world].

[Briefly share a relevant detail that brings this moment to life].


I [achieved specific result] in [short timeframe].


I initially aimed for [modest goal].

Instead I'm [describe where you physically were], and all I can think is:


"[Vulnerable internal question]?"


I realised:


[Core emotional truth/realisation].


[Reframe the result in a humorous or self-deprecating way].


[Pivot to relevant offer or key takeaway].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: How to grow your LinkedIn AND your email list at the same time
description: Lead magnet template that uses a curated resource collection to grow your email list through LinkedIn engagement. Leverages reciprocity psychology by offering free value in exchange for a comment, triggering DM-based delivery. The social proof of collection size and promised outcomes creates urgency while the comment mechanic boosts algorithmic reach.
content:
I spent [time frame] collecting [X] [resources/tools] to help you:

[Desirable outcome 1]
[Desirable outcome 2]
[Desirable outcome 3]

Why?

Well, [relevant external change or shift] has changed in [year].

It's [negative consequence] than ever before to [achieve the thing your audience wants].

So, to help you [desired outcome], I collected all my [proven assets].

These have [impressive social proof result].

Inside [Name of the resource], you'll get:

[Asset 1 + proof number]
[Asset 2]
[Asset 3, etc.]

This is everything you need to [core promise] (for [use case 1] and [use case 2]).

Want FREE access?

Comment "[trigger word]"

I'll send it to you over DM ASAP.

title: A counterintuitive lesson on how to write gut-punching hooks
description: An underdog revenge narrative template that uses jealousy and injustice as a hook to grab attention. Contrasts merit with the one skill that actually gets rewarded, creating a powerful 'aha' moment. The arc from losing out to building something better leverages loss aversion psychology and inspires readers who feel overlooked or undervalued.
content:
[Someone else] got the [thing you deserved] over me.

[Their weakness]. [Their one advantage].

I [proof of your merit]. [More proof]. [More proof].

[He/She] was 10x better than me at [the thing that actually got rewarded].


[He/She] got [reward]. I got "[dismissive quote]."

It took [time frame] for me to click that most [key realisation].

[Brief description of what you built instead].

Now I [the positive outcomes since]. No [negative thing you escaped]

[Key takeaway(s)].

[Inspirational sign-off].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: Is your account growing? If so, re-introduce yourself (every 90 days)
description: Personal brand re-introduction template designed for growing accounts to periodically remind followers who you are. Uses structured transparency to share what you sell, how you market, and what tools you use. The hero's journey from humble beginnings builds relatability while the detailed breakdown positions you as an open book, fostering trust and connection.
content:
I'm [age], [nationality], [personality trait/fact], and run a [brief business descriptor].

Last [time period ago], [specific metric/accolade achieved].

What [we/I] sell:

[List products/services you offer + prices (optional)].

How we [market/sell]:

[Channel/method] [cadence]
[Channel/method] [cadence]
[Channel/method] [cadence]

Tools [we/I] use:

[Tool 1] ([what it does])
[Tool 2] ([what it does])
[Tool 3] ([what it does])

Where I started:

[X] years ago, I was [previous identity] who knew nothing about [relevant skill set]. I just wanted [modest original desire]. Today, I [brief description of where you're at now].

If you do a fraction of this, you can [scaled-down version of the result]. You just need [three simple ingredients].

[Warm sign-off].

title: This *rare* post type is a hype-builder
description: A project-launch announcement template that uses self-deprecating honesty ("should've launched years ago") to disarm skepticism, then builds anticipation by tracing the idea's organic spread. The "signal is clear" pivot reframes a personal launch as audience-demanded, while the specific format bullets and positioning statement create legitimacy without feeling salesy.
content:
I'm finally launching [project type] I should've launched [years/months] ago:

[Project Name].

(Relatable/Self-deprecating aside – optional)

[Collaborator/co-creator – if applicable] and I first started talking about [core concept] in [year].

Since then, the idea's taken on a life of its own. It's shown up in [place 1], [place 2], and [place 3]. [Example of how far idea/concept has already spread].

The signal is clear: [your audience] needs more [resource type] like this. [Specific format 1], [specific format 2], and [specific format 3] from people actually doing the work.

That's what I'll be covering on [the project].

[Name of thing] is built for [specific audience] who want [desirable outcome] without relying on [common tactic you're positioning against].

What to expect:

- [Format detail 1]
- [Format detail 2]
- [Format detail 3]

For now: [What's available right now]. [What's coming next and when].

[Simple CTA: where to find it].

title: How to reach more eyeballs on LinkedIn
description: A mindset-shift template that uses a short list of ready-made phrases to lower the activation energy for trying something uncomfortable. Reframes a common fear as actually being the high-leverage move, then ties the principle into current work to make the advice feel lived-in rather than abstract.
content:
One of the best things you can do for your [area of life/career/system] is get comfortable [doing this specific thing]:

[Ready-made phrase/unconventional path 1]
[Ready-made phrase/unconventional path 2]
[Ready-made phrase/unconventional path 3]
[Ready-made phrase/unconventional path 4]

I used to think [doing this thing] would [negative consequence you feared]. But 99x out of 100, it's actually [positive outcome you experienced].

[Brief personal/professional update that connects to the topic].

And as [context for what you're building/doing now], I'm not looking for [the obvious/expected trait/method]. I'm looking for [people/tools/methods] that [embody what you just advocated for].

title: Are you making this mistake?
description: A listicle of common mistakes that positions you as the expert who sees patterns others miss. Each mistake includes a diagnosis and a fix, giving the reader immediate value. The closing self-reflection prompt raises the stakes by connecting the surface critique to something the reader actually cares about.
content:
I see the same [platform/area] [mistakes/problems] every single day:

- [Mistake 1].
[Why it fails/Good or bad example]. [Practical advice].

- [Mistake 2].
[Why it fails/Good or bad example]. [Practical advice].

- [Mistake 3].
[Why it fails/Good or bad example]. [Practical advice].

- [Mistake 4].
[Why it fails/Good or bad example]. [Practical advice].

- [Mistake 5].
[Why it fails/Good or bad example]. [Practical advice].

Your [the thing you've been critiquing] is your [what's really at stake].
[One sentence elevating why it matters more than they think].

[Direct prompt asking the reader to evaluate their current approach].

Be honest… [Closing question that makes them self-reflect]?

title: The content people are starving for in The Age of AI
description: A problem-reframe template for when the obvious metric looks good but the meaningful one doesn't. Rejects the knee-jerk fix, presents a client-tested alternative as a numbered playbook, and closes with a key takeaway that reframes the real distinction — great for consultants and strategists selling a non-obvious approach.
content:
Your [team/department/strategy] is [driving impressive metric] this year. But your [key person/strategy] is [driving low metric].

The solution isn't to [obvious but wrong approach] because that just [briefly example why it's a mistake].

Instead, [briefly sum up your alternative solution].

A few strategies that I use with [client type]:

1. [Step/Strategy 1]
[Tips/Sub-steps/Questions].

2. [Step/Strategy 2]
[Tips/Sub-steps/Questions].

3. [Step/Strategy 3]
[Tips/Sub-steps/Questions]

Outcomes:

[List benefits or state key desirable outcome].

[Key takeaway that reframes this core distinction].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: Curation is a must for any creator's toolkit
description: A curated-list template that leverages reciprocity and tagging mechanics to maximize reach. The opening hook removes a perceived barrier ("you don't need X"), then hands the reader an immediate shortcut. The "tag someone who deserves to be on this list" closing is a proven engagement driver that doubles the post's algorithmic footprint.
content:
You don't need an [intimidating credential] to [learn/do desirable thing].

These [X] LinkedIn creators make it easy for you:

1. [Name]: [Brief summary of what they do].
2. [Name]: [Brief summary of what they do].
3. [Name]: [Brief summary of what they do].

Tag someone who deserves to be on this list. Who should be #[next number]? Comment below.

title: What your prospective customers actually want
description: A desire-gap template that names a universal want, surfaces the invisible barrier most people never identify, then splits readers into two identities to create self-selection. The closing offer feels earned because it directly solves the gap just revealed.
content:
Many [people/group] want [desirable change/outcome].

But most of them never [identify invisible barrier that holds them back].

[Identity A] [default behaviour pattern].
[Identity B] [upgraded behaviour pattern].

If you're interested in [achieving the desirable outcome], [introduce your offer/resource and how to access it].

title: The boring (but effective) truth about posting on LinkedIn
description: A regret-and-hindsight storytelling template that weaponises the "I ignored good advice" arc. Stacks dismissed advice, shows the cost of ignoring it, then validates the advice through observed proof. The vulnerability creates trust while the relatable human truth lands the lesson.
content:
I [dismissive reaction] at so much [your field/niche] advice.


(And now I wish I'd listened)

When I started, everyone kept saying:

"[Common advice #1]."
"[Common advice #2]."
"[Common advice #3]."

And I thought: yeah, yeah. I'll figure it out my own way.

So I did. [What you actually did – list 2-3 wrong choices/mistakes you made].

It cost me.

The advice I ignored the most: "[The one piece of advice you wish you'd taken, in quote format]."

I thought [why you dismissed it]. That [second reason you dismissed it]. That I needed to [third reason, framed as what you believed instead].

I was wrong.

The [clients/businesses/individuals you now see succeeding]? [Brief description of what they're doing]. They've been [doing the thing you dismissed] for [timeframe]. And it's working.

Because [relatable human truth that explains why the advice works].

[Summarise core lesson].

I just had to learn it the hard way.

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: Justin Welsh's favourite 6-step persuasive post writing framework
description: A six-step persuasive narrative template that opens with a relatable pain, moves through personal struggle with concrete details, pivots to present-day opportunity, then funnels readers toward a single first step and CTA. The emotional arc from empathy to optimism makes the offer feel like a natural next step.
content:
There's nothing worse than [relatable pain point].

But a lot of [people/group] are in that situation. It sucks.

I can remember [when you experienced this yourself].

[Specific details of your struggle: financial, emotional, professional — give concrete numbers and feelings if possible].

Back then, [explain why things were different].

But today? [Explain how the landscape has changed for the better].

- [Opportunity/option]
- [Opportunity/option]
- [Opportunity/option]

[Optimistic one-liner about the opportunity available].

[Briefly acknowledge any difficulties or roadblocks honestly].

But [restate the core benefit of taking action].

If you don't know how to start, [give one simple first step].

I'll show you exactly how [mention your resource/offer/lead magnet].

[Outline the value prop].

[Simple next step - CTA].

title: The "timestamp" hook
description: A timeline-contrast hook template that juxtaposes a past low point with a present achievement in just two lines, then fills in the story between them. The specific years, moments, and milestones create cinematic tension while the closing question turns the narrative into a universal lesson.
content:
[Year]: [Where you struggled or almost gave up]
[Year]: [Where you are now – e.g. same domain, elevated position]

I almost quit [role/pursuit] [timeframe] in. I remember [specific moment of doubt – what you were looking at, thinking, feeling].

I [complained about/struggled with] [specific frustration]. [Another frustration]. [Another frustration].

[Optional: Self-deprecating joke or aside that lightens the weight of the story].

[What you tried: effort you put in that still didn't work, 2–3 short lines].

Still [briefly explain where you were at mentally].

So before [pivotal moment], I made [decision you'd come to].

[Briefly expand on what happened: who was there, their relationship to you, where you were, what you said/did].

What [they said/happened] next [impactful statement]:
"[Question/advice/moment that changed your perspective]."

[Share what you realised].

[The initial change you made/action you took].

[Short timeframe later], [the first result – specific number or milestone]. I still remember the feeling of [what that felt like].

[X years/months] later, I've [stacked accomplishments – numbers, programs, milestones], and I'm [doing the elevated thing from your hook].

So.. before you [quit/give up on] something this year, [ask yourself/remember this]:

[Repeat/reinforce core quote/lesson].

[Key takeaway].

title: If you're seeing this right now, you're ahead of 99% of people
description: An urgency-and-opportunity template that frames a current trend as a once-in-a-generation moment. Uses bold comparisons and vivid predictions to build FOMO, segments the audience with targeted actions, then provides a simple starter playbook to convert excitement into action.
content:
We are staring at the BIGGEST opportunity since [recent reference point] and [historical reference point]. If you're seeing this right now, you're ahead of [large percentage] of [people/group].

This isn't just another [category] post.

For context:

[Name of tool/trend] is [briefly explain what it does/what's going on].

If you [take relevant action], then [bold claim about what becomes possible].

I can see a future where [vivid, specific prediction].

[Briefly explain how the reader should think about this].

[Audience segment 1]: you need to [specific action].

[Audience segment 2 – if relevant]: [specific action].

Here's how you should get started:

- [Step 1]
- [Step 2]
- [Step 3]

[Sum up opportunity/share key takeaway].

title: How to write a viral origin story
description: A chronological age-by-age origin story template that traces a journey through wins, losses, and pivots. The stacked timeline creates narrative tension that resolves into a universal lesson—specificity builds credibility while the summary close transforms personal history into practical wisdom.
content:
Age [X]: [Origin story / starting point].
Age [X]: [Early attempt]. [Result].
Age [X]: [Failure or setback with a memorable detail].
Age [X]: [First meaningful win]. [Specific result].
Age [X]: [Loss or humbling moment]. [Why it happened].
Age [X]: [Win/Loss/Obstacle].
Age [X]: [Win/Loss/Obstacle].
Age [X]: [Win/Loss/Obstacle].
Age [X]: [Win/Loss/Obstacle].
Age [X]: [Win/Loss/Obstacle].
Age [X]: [Peak outcome - the big result].
Age [X]: [Where you are now & current metric].
Age [X]: [Recent win].


[Summarise key takeaway].

[Practical lesson].

[Brief motivational close].

title: Surface-level vs basement-level problems (The Generic Content Trap)
description: A surface-level-vs-depth reframe template that opens with a provocative quote, surfaces a relatable frustration cycle, then names the hidden skill that solves it. The 'comment keyword' CTA builds engagement signals for algorithmic reach while making the offer feel generous.
content:
I can't stop thinking about a [piece of content] I saw the other day.

It said:

"[Quoted insight]."

This is so true.

But even after we [take the first step], we [run into obstacle]:

[Step 1 of relatable cycle].
[Step 2 of relatable cycle].
[Step 3 of relatable cycle].

[Summarise reframe - link this obstacle to quoted insight].

But realize this:


[Redefine obstacle in simple terms].

[Expand on why it's actually achievable].

The problem?

[Name the hidden obstacle that makes people give up].

So... you [expand on negative consequences].

The skill to build is being able to [action] despite being surrounded by:

[Obstacle 1]
[Obstacle 2]
[Obstacle 3]

If you can build this skill, you can [achieve the desirable outcome].

So [take logical next step] now.

And realise [key thing to bear in mind].

For me, I've [pull from your own personal experience].

And my skill as a [role] accelerated the fastest when I [specific turning point].

I [brief list of relevant social proof points or results you've achieved].

But if I was just starting over, I'd [take specific path].

So I created [lead magnet name].

It has everything you need to [become/achieve something specific]:

[Feature 1 with benefit]
[Feature 2 with benefit]
[Feature 3 with benefit]

And more.

Want access so you can [desirable outcome]?

Comment "[keyword]" and I'll DM it to you for free.

(And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "[keyword]" and I'll send it over.)

title: How to test an unusual offer (without pitching)
description: A soft-pitch offer template that names an unconventional role without using sales language. The self-aware aside and casual tone disarm skepticism while the transformation arc (barrier → breakthrough) does the selling. Ends with an open question to invite conversation rather than close a hard sell.
content:
A [role/friend/client] paid me [specific price] to be their [unexpected, informal role].


Not their [expected professional role 1].


Not their [expected professional role 2].


Their [repeat unexpected role].


(Ok [they/he/she] didn't call it a "[unexpected role]" – [self-aware aside that acknowledges the absurdity])


Even the [most impressive version of target audience] [vivid description of what they do when they encounter core problem].


[Briefly sum up why the problem persists].


But [Unexpected role] won't let you [avoid it].


[She's/He's] [purpose of role].


Once you [get past the initial barrier], [positive transformation that occurs].


If you ask me, it's [casual verdict].

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: Why most case study posts fail to convert
description: A high-conversion case study template that leads with a specific client result, then unpacks the anti-pattern thinking that made it possible. The mistake-vs-solution structure builds credibility before the program launch pitch. Stacked bonuses with anchored pricing create urgency without discounting the core offer.
content:
Merely [timeframe] after joining [your program/offer]...


[Client name] [achieved a specific, impressive result].


This is the kind of result [we/I] could only dream of when [we/I] first launched [program name].


Now, after [working with X+ people] across [timeframe/cohorts/rounds], [We/I] know this is achievable for anyone who follows this system.

[Client name] earned that [result] because [he/she] went against the grain.

[He/She] worked hard to avoid the most common pitfalls:

[Common mistake 1].
[Common mistake 2].
[Common mistake 3].

These are mistakes we see [target audience] making far too often on [platform/in their field].

[Contrasting positive behaviour the client adopted].

In [client name]'s words:

"[Short, compelling testimonial quote]."

What [client name] learned is what [we/I] hope to teach everyone who goes through [program name]:

How to [unlock key outcome 1]
How to [unlock key outcome 2]
And how to [unlock key outcome 3]

So, if you're a [target audience] struggling with [core problem], this is your opportunity to [desirable transformation].

[Program name] officially [starts/launches] on [date]!

We still have [early bird resources] for [target audience] that act now...

And we have a brand new bonus stack, which is the best we have ever done:

[Bonus 1] ([anchored price]): [One-line description]
[Bonus 2] ([anchored price]): [One-line description]
[Bonus 3] ([anchored price]): [One-line description]

And [we're/I'm] giving them to you for FREE.

(That's over [total anchored value] worth of exclusive resources...)

But these bonuses are only available to [early applicants/group].

Spots are filling up quickly.

And there are just [number] days left, and [urgency/credibility statement].

Click here to learn more: [CTA with link]

[Question to foster engagement]?

title: Let's be blunt about LinkedIn for a sec
description: A structured 3-step framework template that opens with a credibility-building result achieved without conventional constraints. The 'Check:' sub-points add practical depth without padding. The TLDR close distils the framework into a pithy takeaway, making the post shareable across audiences with different reading habits.
content:
Last week I had a big realisation after [achieving impressive result] (without [conventional constraint/method]):

1. [First stage of framework]

[Briefly expand on what stage entails].

Check: [Key consideration].

2. [Second stage of framework]

[Briefly expand on what stage entails].

Check: [Key consideration].

3. [Third stage of framework]

[Briefly expand on what stage entails].

Check: [Key consideration].

TLDR:

[Summarise core idea behind framework].

[Pithy takeaway].

title: Ironic Realisation
description: Exposes hidden advantages or privileges within professional categories to create relatability and engagement. Uses humor to soften potentially controversial observations while building connection with audiences who share similar frustrations or realizations about industry inequalities.
content:
When you realise most ‘[category advisors]‘ are [description of unspoken privileges]...

You [humorous reaction].

title: A Gift Gone Wrong?
description: Creates engaging storytelling posts by setting up an unexpected twist around a gift or gesture. Uses curiosity gap technique to hook readers with "Interestingly..." cliffhanger. Perfect for sharing surprising outcomes, lessons learned, or memorable moments that demonstrate insights about human nature or business.
content:
So this [time period] I gave [individual] a [thoughtful/unusual gift or item], but when [they interacted with it], [something unexpected happened].

Interestingly…

title: Unconventional Strategy
description: Leverages curiosity and specificity to attract professionals seeking innovative solutions. The "unconventional" angle creates intrigue while promising concrete examples and mindset shifts. Perfect for thought leaders sharing unique approaches or tools in their industry.
content:
How to use [unconventional tool/strategy] in your [professional context] 

(X examples that will change the way you think about [tool/strategy])

title: Never Again
description: Creates contrarian posts by rejecting conventional wisdom to spark debate and engagement. Works by positioning yourself against popular practices while sharing personal negative experiences. Leverages controversy and relatability to drive comments and discussions from audiences who either agree or disagree.
content:
I'm done with [traditional or commonly accepted practice]. 

Every time I [engage in this practice], [describe the negative outcome or 
reaction].

title: What My Wins Taught Me
description: A numbered list format that builds credibility by connecting specific learnings to measurable outcomes. Uses social proof and concrete timeframes to establish expertise while providing actionable insights. The parenthetical accomplishments create immediate value perception and encourage engagement through curiosity about proven results.
content: X [content types] I learned during [specific time] (these helped me [list relevant accomplishments]):

title: How to imply you’re an expert
description: This template uses storytelling and pattern interruption to challenge conventional wisdom. Opens with a relatable anecdote, reveals an unexpected outcome, then pivots to question common strategies your audience follows. Builds credibility through narrative while positioning your contrarian viewpoint.
content:
I remember a [person/scenario] who/that used to [supportive/beneficial action].

[Person/Scenario] used to [key action].

But then [things stopped or resulted in a negative outcome].

It's because [prior action/event that initiated this change].

[Briefly expand on what happened to conclude the story].

See a lot of [target audience] subscribe to the [common strategy/action]. 

But here’s the thing…

[Give your unique perspective/challenge how this strategy/action is implemented].

[Statement that ties your perspective back to the initial story].

[Key takeaway].

title: Don’t do this… Instead, do this…
description: This template uses contrast psychology to establish expertise by first highlighting common mistakes, then providing superior alternatives. Perfect for sharing lessons learned from recent experiences while positioning yourself as a knowledgeable problem solver in your field.
content:
This week, I [experienced a remarkable/unusual event].

Here are [X] ways I wouldn't recommend to [related activity]:

1. [Common ineffective approach 1]

[Explain why it's ineffective]

2. [Common ineffective approach 2]

[Explain why it's ineffective]

3. [Common ineffective approach 3]

[Explain why it's ineffective]

This is how I'd do it instead:

1. [Effective strategy 1]. [Brief explanation].

2. [Effective strategy 2]. [Brief explanation].

3. [Effective strategy 3]. [Brief explanation].

Remember: 

[Takeaway that summarises why this advice is important]

title: If you’re an avid learner, try this…
description: Establishes credibility through curated wisdom, drives engagement via relatable lessons, and creates series potential. Uses social proof and curiosity gap to boost reach. Ideal for thought leaders sharing actionable insights from research or experience.
content:
I've read [X]+ books on [topic]. Here are [X] of the most important lessons I have learned and applied to my [life/business/scenario]:

1. "[Quote/Lesson 1]."
([Author] - [Book title])

2. "[Quote/Lesson 2]."
([Author] - [Book title])

3. "[Quote/Lesson 3]."
([Author] - [Book title])

I have [X] more. Should I do a part 2?

[Question for audience or call to action].

title: Why Most Fall Short
description: Creates intrigue by highlighting exclusivity and challenging common assumptions about why most people fail to achieve desired outcomes. Uses scarcity psychology and contrarian positioning to hook readers who want to discover the real reasons behind success versus failure.
content:
Only [small percentage] of [profession/group] achieve [desired result].

And it’s not because they [lack traits/fail to take necessary action].

title: The Nonconformist
description: Creates contrarian content by positioning yourself against popular advice or beliefs. Uses cognitive dissonance to grab attention and establish thought leadership. Perfect for challenging industry norms, sharing unconventional wisdom, or differentiating your perspective from mainstream thinking.
content:
Them: [Common advice or belief] 

Me: [Contradictory action or opinion]

title: Listen, You Need This…
description: Creates urgency by addressing a specific audience's critical need while dismissing surface-level motivations. Uses direct address and contradiction to grab attention, then builds anticipation with "Because..." to deliver deeper, more compelling reasoning that resonates with the target group's real concerns.
content:
[Target audience]: You need [critical element].

And not just because [superficial reason].

title: Sticking With It
description: Opens with a relatable problem to hook readers, then immediately provides a specific, quantified solution. Uses the pain/relief psychological principle by first acknowledging struggle, then offering concrete value through measurable time or effort savings. Perfect for positioning expertise while helping others.
content:
Struggling to [achieve desired outcome]?

(This [solution/tool/process] saves me [X amount of time/effort] each [time frame].)

title: A Vital Cog
description: Establishes credibility through firsthand experience while showcasing dramatic transformation results. Uses social proof and insider knowledge to capture attention. Perfect for consultants, employees, or collaborators wanting to highlight successful projects they contributed to and build trust with their audience.
content:
[Company/project] went from [starting point] to [impressive result] in just [timeframe].

How do I know?

Because I was [directly involved/in a contributing role].

title: All Top 1% Creators Understand This
description: A storytelling template that builds emotional connection through contrast and resolution. Uses a memorable figure's journey from challenge to success, highlighting key decisions and their lasting impact. Engages audiences through relatable struggles and universal lessons, ending with reflection prompts.
content:
A story [from someone significant or memorable].

If you’re [a relevant audience], this might resonate with you as it did with me.

They said, [insert a quote or a poignant statement that introduces the theme of the story].

They’ve always [share a notable quality or value of the person].

But they remember this…

It was [describe a situation or challenge they faced, focusing on the tension or stakes].

They could just picture [describe the impact of the situation, using relatable or emotional imagery].

[Detail an action they took to resolve the situation, emphasising the decision-making process or sacrifice].

At the time, [introduce a contrasting perspective or person to heighten the significance of the actions].

[Describe the contrasting perspective’s outcomes, underscoring the lesson or takeaway].

And in all these years, [highlight the enduring positive outcomes of the initial decision].

- [Mission/Aim 2]

Uses cognitive reframing to shift mindset from victim to empowered individual. Perfect for addressing career setbacks, rejection, or criticism.

title: Most people play it FAR too safe with their content
description: Dispels myths about success by sharing personal failures and unexpected outcomes. Uses fear deflation and pattern recognition to show that obstacles rarely materialize as imagined. Motivates action by revealing what inaction actually costs and reframing perceived barriers as unnecessary.

That's the uncomfortable truth most people never figure out.

[Personal admission of the mistake or false belief you held for years]. [What that looked like in practice, what you were waiting for or avoiding].

[The moment that revealed nothing happened / nobody cared / the fear was unfounded].

But [surprising and beneficial thing you noticed].

[Describe the specific fears or obstacles you anticipated - name 2-4 concretely].

[Single-line deflation: None of that happened / It never came / The response was silence].

[Give each feared obstacle a specific, mundane reason it never materialised].

I’ve watched this play out with [clients / people I coach / groups I’ve observed]. [Explain the pattern you observed, describe the behaviour - eg. the over-preparing, the polishing, the delay].

[Describe what actually happens when they finally take beneficial action].

[Name the realisation that hits. What did the fear actually cost them, and what was it protecting them from?]

[Reframe the broader system or environment - what’s actually going on?]

But if you decide to [take specific action]? If you decide to [take the hard route]? [Summarise what will actually happen].

[Reinforce with a second personal proof point – a specific decision you made, the judgment you expected, and the reality of what followed].

The people who [achieve desirable outcome] aren’t [positive trait] than everyone else. They just figured out that [sum up key realisation].

You don't need [perceived requisite 1]. You don't need [perceived requisite 2]. You don't need [perceived requisite 3].

You just need to start [key next action(s)].

[Question for audience or call to action].

description: This template leverages the "rags to riches" narrative to build credibility and inspire action. By sharing humble beginnings and specific struggles, it creates relatability and emotional connection. The psychological principle is social proof through personal transformation, showing others that success is achievable regardless of starting point.
content:

[Share 1-2 details of living circumstances and/or location]

X years ago, I decided to [pursue a goal].

I/We didn’t have [an advantage].

I/We didn’t even know what a [common means to achieve goal] was.

By [Distill main action or principle into a short concept].

title: Hidden Barrier To Success
description: Highlights overlooked details that create major impact by sharing a personal struggle story. Uses contrast between "small" and significant outcomes to grab attention, then builds credibility through vulnerability. Perfect for coaches, consultants, and professionals wanting to emphasize attention to detail.
content:
[Seemingly small detail] does matter.

Early in my [journey], I struggled to [achieve goal]. [Primary barrier that held you back].

title: Unsure how to structure your next LinkedIn post? Try this…
description: A contrarian expertise template that positions you as an authority by correcting common misconceptions. Uses problem/solution structure with mistake identification and actionable fixes. Leverages social proof through "common mistakes" framing to build credibility and trust.
content:
Most [common activity/assets] I see are [negative descriptor].

[Key insight].

[Mistake]

title: Shocking? Yes, But Effective
description: Creates engagement through storytelling by opening with an intriguing outcome then revealing the journey. Uses curiosity gap psychology to hook readers who want to understand how the surprising result happened. Perfect for sharing unexpected business wins, career pivots, or counterintuitive strategies.
content:
My [colleague/peer/company] [took surprising action that led to specific outcome].

[Share where it started - give brief details on any significant events that led up to your surprising experience].

title: Everyone Should X (Here’s How)
description: Creates authority by positioning you as the solution to a common problem. Uses aspiration (everyone should achieve X) followed by scarcity (most don't know how) to build tension, then offers your proven method as the bridge. Leverages problem/solution psychology and social proof.
content:
